puzzles.headline: Nelson Mandela Released From Prison
puzzles.description: After 27 years behind bars, the world's most famous political prisoner walked free — electrifying a nation on the brink of transformation.

tuesday: The Berlin Wall had fallen just months before
South Africa was still three years from its first democratic election

puzzles.context_card: Nelson Mandela had spent 18 of his 27 years imprisoned on Robben Island, a bleak outcrop in Table Bay. By the time he walked free on February 11th, 1990, he had become the most famous political prisoner in the world. Within four years, he was president.

puzzles.headline: The Apollo 11 Moon Landing
puzzles.description: Two astronauts left bootprints in lunar dust while 600 million people watched on flickering television screens back home.

tuesday: Woodstock was just weeks away
The Boeing 747 had not yet carried a single passenger

puzzles.context_card: Neil Armstrong's first steps on the Moon were watched by an estimated 600 million people — one-fifth of the world's population. The lunar module was called Eagle. Armstrong had only 30 seconds of fuel remaining when he found a landing spot. He called it 'magnificent desolation.'

puzzles.headline: The Titanic Sinks on Maiden Voyage
puzzles.description: The "unsinkable" ocean liner struck an iceberg in the North Atlantic, taking more than 1,500 souls with her to the bottom.

tuesday: The Panama Canal was still under construction
Fenway Park opened the same week

puzzles.context_card: The Titanic was carrying some of the world's wealthiest people and hundreds of immigrants seeking a new life in America. It struck the iceberg at 11:40 PM and sank in under three hours. The nearby RMS Carpathia rescued 705 survivors. The disaster led to major changes in maritime safety regulations.

puzzles.event_formal: Assassination of President John F. Kennedy
puzzles.headline: JFK Assassinated in Dallas
puzzles.description: A motorcade through Dealey Plaza ended the presidency and fractured a nation's sense of innocence in a matter of seconds.

wednesday: Martin Luther King Jr. had just delivered his most famous speech that summer
The Beatles would land in America for the first time within three months

puzzles.context_card: Lee Harvey Oswald fired three shots from the sixth floor of the Texas School Book Depository on November 22nd, 1963. Kennedy was pronounced dead at 1:00 PM. Two days later, Oswald was shot by nightclub owner Jack Ruby in the Dallas Police Department basement — live on national television. The truth of what happened that day has never fully left public debate.

puzzles.headline: World Wide Web Invented
puzzles.description: A British physicist at CERN proposed a system of interlinked hypertext documents — and quietly set the stage for the modern internet.

wednesday: The Exxon Valdez had just run aground in Alaska
Nintendo's Game Boy went on sale that same spring

puzzles.context_card: Tim Berners-Lee's original proposal at CERN was returned with the note: 'Vague, but exciting.' He wanted a way for physicists to share documents across different computers. The first web page went live in 1991, and Berners-Lee never patented his invention — he gave it away for free, insisting the web should belong to everyone.

puzzles.headline: Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster
puzzles.description: A botched safety test at a Soviet power plant in Ukraine triggered the worst nuclear accident in history, spreading fallout across Europe.

wednesday: The Space Shuttle Challenger had been lost earlier that same year
Maradona's 'Hand of God' goal happened weeks later at the World Cup

puzzles.context_card: On April 26, 1986, Reactor 4 at Chernobyl exploded during a low-power test. The fire released radioactive material 9 kilometers into the atmosphere. Soviet authorities evacuated Pripyat (population 49,000) 36 hours later. The final death toll remains disputed — from 31 immediate deaths to estimates of thousands of cancer cases from radiation exposure. The destroyed reactor was finally enclosed in a giant steel sarcophagus in 2016.

puzzles.event: D-Day: Allied Forces Land at Normandy
puzzles.headline: D-Day: Allied Invasion of Normandy
puzzles.description: More than 150,000 troops crossed the English Channel in the largest amphibious assault ever attempted, storming five beaches at dawn.

thursday: Roosevelt was serving an unprecedented third term as US president

puzzles.context_card: On June 6th, 1944, the Allies launched the most complex military operation in history. Paratroopers had already dropped behind enemy lines by the time the first landing craft hit the beaches at 6:30 AM. The cost was enormous — over 4,000 Allied soldiers died on D-Day alone — but within a year, Germany had surrendered.

puzzles.headline: First iPhone Released
puzzles.description: Steve Jobs pulled a sleek rectangle from his pocket onstage in San Francisco and casually redefined what a phone could be.

thursday: The final Harry Potter book was published that same summer

puzzles.context_card: Steve Jobs unveiled the iPhone on January 9, 2007, calling it 'a revolutionary product.' It didn't go on sale until June, but it immediately changed what people expected from mobile devices. The initial model had no App Store — that came in 2008. By 2011, the iPhone had made Apple the most valuable company in the world.

puzzles.headline: The Watergate Break-In
puzzles.description: Five men were caught burglarizing the Democratic National Committee headquarters, setting off a scandal that would consume the presidency.

thursday: Nixon had just returned from his historic visit to China that same year

puzzles.context_card: The five men arrested inside the Democratic National Committee headquarters at 2:30 AM were carrying wiretapping equipment and $2,300 in sequential hundred-dollar bills. A security guard named Frank Wills noticed tape on a door lock and called the police. What began as a third-rate burglary unraveled into the greatest political scandal in American history, ending a presidency two years later.

puzzles.event: The Atomic Bomb Drops on Hiroshima
puzzles.headline: Atomic Bomb Dropped on Hiroshima
puzzles.description: A single bomb obliterated a Japanese city in seconds, ushering humanity into the nuclear age with a blinding flash visible for miles.

friday: Mussolini had been executed by partisans just months earlier

puzzles.context_card: The Manhattan Project had cost two billion dollars and employed hundreds of thousands of people — most of whom had no idea what they were building. On August 6th, 1945, the result was dropped on Hiroshima. Three days later, a second bomb fell on Nagasaki. Japan surrendered on August 15th. The atomic age had begun.

puzzles.headline: Fall of the Soviet Union
puzzles.description: The red flag came down over the Kremlin for the last time, ending a superpower and redrawing the map of Eurasia overnight.

friday: The World Wide Web had just become publicly available that August

puzzles.context_card: On December 26, 1991, the Supreme Soviet of the USSR ratified the Belovezh Accords, formally ending the Soviet Union. What had been the world's largest country by area splintered into 15 independent nations. For 69 years, the Soviet experiment had shaped global politics. Its end brought both hope for a new world order and anxiety about nuclear weapons in unstable new states.

puzzles.event_formal: Wright Brothers' First Powered Flight at Kitty Hawk
puzzles.headline: The Wright Brothers' First Flight
puzzles.description: On a windy stretch of North Carolina sand, two bicycle mechanics from Ohio kept a powered aircraft aloft for twelve glorious seconds.

friday: The first Tour de France was held that same summer

puzzles.context_card: The Wright Brothers made four flights at Kitty Hawk on December 17th, 1903. The first lasted just 12 seconds and covered 120 feet. By the end of that day, they had flown a total of 59 seconds. Orville would later say that the sensation of flight never fully wore off on him — it just kept getting better.

puzzles.headline: Lindbergh Flies Solo Across the Atlantic
puzzles.description: A lanky 25-year-old from Minnesota landed in Paris after 33 hours alone in a single-engine monoplane, becoming the most famous person on Earth.

saturday: Jazz ruled the speakeasies and hemlines were rising

puzzles.context_card: The Spirit of St. Louis was so loaded with fuel at takeoff that it barely cleared the telephone wires at the end of the runway. Charles Lindbergh landed at Le Bourget at 10:22 PM on May 21st, 1927, and was immediately mobbed by the crowd. He became the most famous man in the world overnight — and remained so for the rest of his life, for better and worse.

puzzles.headline: The Cuban Missile Crisis
puzzles.description: For thirteen days the world held its breath as Washington and Moscow edged toward nuclear war over Soviet weapons stationed 90 miles from Florida.

saturday: Marilyn Monroe had sung 'Happy Birthday' to the president that summer

puzzles.context_card: For thirteen days in October 1962, the world came closer to nuclear war than it ever has before or since. Soviet ships carrying additional missiles turned back at the US naval blockade. Soviet leader Khrushchev eventually agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a US pledge not to invade Cuba and a secret agreement to remove American missiles from Turkey. The crisis was resolved — but only barely.

puzzles.event: Rosa Parks Refuses to Give Up Her Seat
puzzles.event_formal: Rosa Parks' Bus Protest, Montgomery, Alabama
puzzles.headline: Rosa Parks Refuses to Give Up Her Seat
puzzles.description: A seamstress in Montgomery, Alabama declined to move to the back of a city bus, igniting a 381-day boycott that changed the nation.

saturday: Rock and roll was new, Disneyland had just opened its gates

puzzles.context_card: Rosa Parks was not simply tired from work when she refused to move — she was tired of giving in. She had been trained at the Highlander Folk School, a center for civil rights activism. Her arrest on December 1st, 1955 was strategic: the NAACP had been looking for a test case. The resulting boycott lasted 381 days and ended with the Supreme Court ruling bus segregation unconstitutional.

puzzles.headline: The Challenger Disaster
puzzles.description: A space shuttle broke apart 73 seconds after launch as millions — including schoolchildren watching live — looked on in horror.
puzzles.context_card: Engineers at Morton Thiokol had warned NASA the night before launch that O-rings could fail in temperatures below 53°F — and it was 28°F at Cape Canaveral that morning. Their objections were overruled. The Rogers Commission that investigated the disaster famously included physicist Richard Feynman, who demonstrated the O-ring failure with a glass of ice water at a press conference.

puzzles.headline: The September 11 Attacks
puzzles.description: Hijacked airliners struck the World Trade Center and the Pentagon on a clear Tuesday morning, reshaping American life and global politics.
puzzles.context_card: On September 11, 2001, nearly 3,000 people were killed in a series of coordinated terrorist attacks. The FBI quickly opened an investigation called PENTTBOM. Within days, President George W. Bush had signed the USA PATRIOT Act. The attacks reshaped American foreign policy, airport security, and domestic surveillance in ways still felt today.

puzzles.headline: Attack on Pearl Harbor
puzzles.description: Japanese aircraft swarmed a naval base in Hawaii early on a Sunday morning, sinking battleships and pulling a reluctant nation into world war.
puzzles.context_card: The attack on Pearl Harbor lasted just under two hours but destroyed or damaged 19 Navy ships and 328 aircraft. Of the 2,403 Americans killed, nearly half died aboard the USS Arizona, which sank in nine minutes. Japan had intended to deliver a formal declaration of war before the attack, but the message arrived late — turning a military strike into something Americans would never forgive.

puzzles.headline: First Human in Space
puzzles.description: A Soviet cosmonaut orbited the Earth in 108 minutes, beating the Americans to the ultimate Cold War trophy and becoming an instant global celebrity.

monday: The Berlin Wall went up later that same summer
A new American president had just taken office promising a New Frontier

puzzles.context_card: Yuri Gagarin orbited Earth in Vostok 1 on April 12, 1961, completing one orbit in 108 minutes. At 27, he became the first human to see Earth from space. He parachuted from his capsule before it landed — ejection at 7km was standard procedure. A city in Kazakhstan was renamed Baikonur in his honor, and his face became a symbol of Soviet achievement.

puzzles.event_formal: Storming of the Bastille, French Revolution
puzzles.headline: French Revolution Begins
puzzles.description: A Parisian mob stormed a medieval fortress-prison, toppling a symbol of royal tyranny and launching a decade of radical upheaval across Europe.

monday: George Washington had just been inaugurated as the first US president
Mozart was composing his last operas in Vienna

puzzles.context_card: On July 14, 1789, several hundred Parisians stormed the Bastille, a medieval fortress that had become a symbol of royal tyranny. They freed the seven prisoners inside and killed the governor. The event became the defining moment of the French Revolution and is now commemorated annually as Bastille Day, France's national holiday.

puzzles.headline: Declaration of Independence Signed
puzzles.description: Delegates from thirteen colonies affixed their names to a document declaring self-governance — committing what the Crown would call treason.

monday: Adam Smith published The Wealth of Nations the same year
Captain Cook was on his third and final voyage

puzzles.context_card: Thomas Jefferson drafted the Declaration of Independence in seventeen days. Congress made 86 changes to his draft, cutting roughly a quarter of the text — including a passage condemning the slave trade. The final document was signed by 56 men, many of whom knew they were signing what could become their death warrant.

puzzles.headline: MLK's "I Have a Dream" Speech
puzzles.description: Before a quarter-million people gathered at the Lincoln Memorial, a Baptist preacher from Atlanta delivered the defining oration of the civil rights movement.

tuesday: The Beatles released 'Please Please Me' earlier that year
A nuclear test ban treaty was signed that same summer

puzzles.context_card: The March on Washington was organized in just two months. King's most famous lines — 'I have a dream' — were partly improvised; gospel singer Mahalia Jackson shouted 'Tell them about the dream, Martin!' from behind him on the stage. The prepared text had no mention of dreams.

puzzles.headline: First Email Sent
puzzles.description: An engineer in Cambridge, Massachusetts transmitted a short test message between two computers sitting ten feet apart — and invented a new way for humanity to communicate.

tuesday: Intel had just released its first microprocessor
The Pentagon Papers were published in The New York Times that same year

puzzles.context_card: Ray Tomlinson sent the first email in 1971, choosing the @ symbol to separate the user name from the computer name. The message itself was largely forgettable — something like 'QWERTYUIOP.' But the @ sign became Tomlinson's lasting contribution to digital communication, now used in billions of email addresses worldwide.

puzzles.headline: Google Founded
puzzles.description: Two Stanford PhD students launched a search engine from a rented garage in Menlo Park, convinced they could organize all the world's information.

tuesday: The Lewinsky scandal dominated American headlines
Titanic was the year's biggest film at the box office

puzzles.context_card: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met at Stanford in 1995 and initially didn't get along. Their search algorithm, PageRank, ranked websites by their links rather than keywords. Their first office was a garage rented from a friend. The name 'Google' came from 'googol,' a mathematical term for 1 followed by 100 zeros.

puzzles.headline: Amazon Founded
puzzles.description: A former Wall Street hedge fund VP started an online bookstore out of his garage in Bellevue, Washington, betting big on the nascent internet.

wednesday: Nelson Mandela had just been elected president of South Africa
The first PlayStation launched in Japan that December

puzzles.context_card: Jeff Bezos left his hedge fund job in 1994 to start an online bookstore, originally called Cadabra. He chose the name Amazon because it sounded 'exotic and different,' and the Amazon River was the biggest river he could think of. He raised $1 million from his own parents — the minimum viable investment to start. By 2020, Amazon had a market cap of $1.7 trillion.

puzzles.event_formal: Discovery of the DNA Double Helix
puzzles.headline: DNA Double Helix Discovered
puzzles.description: Two scientists at Cambridge University pieced together the twisted-ladder architecture of life itself, working from a crucial X-ray photograph they didn't take.

wednesday: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay summited Everest weeks later
Elizabeth II had just been crowned Queen of England

puzzles.context_card: Watson and Crick published their double helix model in Nature on April 25, 1953. Their famous line: 'It has not escaped our notice that the specific pairing we have postulated immediately suggests a possible copying mechanism for the genetic material.' Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51, which showed the helix structure, was critical — but she died in 1958 and never shared the Nobel Prize.

puzzles.headline: HIV/AIDS First Identified
puzzles.description: The CDC reported a mysterious cluster of pneumonia cases among young men in Los Angeles, the first public signal of an epidemic that would reshape medicine and culture.

wednesday: MTV broadcast its first music video that August
The IBM personal computer went on sale for the first time

puzzles.context_card: The CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report on June 5, 1981, documented five young gay men in Los Angeles with a rare pneumonia. By the end of 1981, there were 159 reported cases. The disease wouldn't be called AIDS until 1982, and the HIV virus wouldn't be identified until 1984. It would eventually kill 35 million people worldwide.

puzzles.headline: First Human Heart Transplant
puzzles.description: A South African surgeon in Cape Town replaced a dying man's heart with a donor's — a three-hour operation that captivated the world.

thursday: The Summer of Love had just swept through San Francisco

puzzles.context_card: Christiaan Barnard performed the first human heart transplant in Cape Town on December 3, 1967, replacing the heart of Louis Washkansky with that of a young woman killed in a car accident. The operation took nine hours. Washkansky survived just eighteen days before dying of pneumonia — but Barnard had proved it could be done, and within a year, more than a hundred transplants followed worldwide.

puzzles.headline: Polio Vaccine Announced
puzzles.description: A University of Pittsburgh researcher declared his vaccine "safe, effective, and potent," ending years of summertime dread for parents across America.

thursday: Disneyland opened its gates in Anaheim that same summer

puzzles.context_card: When Jonas Salk's polio vaccine was declared 'safe, effective, and potent' on April 12, 1955, church bells rang across America. Polio had paralyzed an average of 35,000 people each year during the early 1950s. Salk famously refused to patent the vaccine — when asked who owned it, he replied, 'The people. Could you patent the sun?'

puzzles.headline: Penicillin Discovered
puzzles.description: A Scottish bacteriologist returned from holiday to find mold killing bacteria in a petri dish he'd left by an open window — a lucky accident that would save millions of lives.

thursday: Mickey Mouse made his first appearance on screen that same year

puzzles.context_card: Alexander Fleming returned from holiday to find a petri dish contaminated by a mold that had killed the surrounding bacteria. He nearly threw it away. It took another decade and two other scientists — Florey and Chain — to turn his observation into a usable drug. By D-Day, there was enough penicillin to treat every wounded Allied soldier on the beaches of Normandy.

puzzles.headline: Instagram Launched
puzzles.description: A photo-sharing app with retro filters launched to an iPhone-only audience and gained 25,000 users on its first day, turning square-cropped snapshots into a cultural language.

friday: The iPad had just debuted earlier that same year

puzzles.context_card: The app gained 25,000 users on its first day — before it even existed on Android. Built in eight weeks, it nearly launched under the name 'Burbn' before a pivot stripped everything except photo filters and sharing. Within two years, a billion-dollar acquisition made it the most expensive purchase-per-employee in tech history.

puzzles.headline: YouTube Founded
puzzles.description: Three former PayPal employees registered a domain for sharing video clips online. The first upload — a 19-second clip filmed at the San Diego Zoo — hardly hinted at what was coming.

friday: Hurricane Katrina footage flooded the platform months after launch

puzzles.context_card: The first video ever uploaded — 18 seconds of a man at the San Diego Zoo — set the tone for what would become the world's second-largest search engine. Three former payment-company employees built it above a pizzeria, and within 18 months sold it for $1.65 billion. The site now serves over a billion hours of video daily, more than broadcast television and Netflix combined.

puzzles.headline: Wikipedia Launched
puzzles.description: A free online encyclopedia invited anyone with an internet connection to write and edit its articles, betting that collective knowledge could rival centuries-old reference institutions.

friday: The dot-com bubble had just burst, leaving server space cheap

puzzles.context_card: Its founder originally envisioned an expert-written encyclopedia called Nupedia, which produced exactly 12 articles in its first year. The pivot to open editing was meant as a feeder project, but within weeks the wiki had outpaced its parent. A 2005 Nature study found its science articles contained roughly the same error rate as Encyclopædia Britannica.

puzzles.headline: Euro Currency Introduced
puzzles.description: Twelve European nations abandoned centuries of national currencies for a shared coin — the most ambitious monetary experiment since Bretton Woods.

saturday: A millennium's last quiet revolution, sealed before the champagne corks flew

puzzles.context_card: The Euro was introduced as an accounting currency on January 1, 1999, with physical notes and coins following on January 1, 2002. Twelve countries abandoned their national currencies — including the Deutsche Mark, French Franc, and Italian Lira — for the first time in history. The transition was initially chaotic in some countries, with ATM glitches and confusion at stores.

puzzles.headline: Nintendo Game Boy Released
puzzles.description: A handheld console with a pea-green monochrome screen and a bundled puzzle game outsold every technologically superior rival, proving that portability trumped pixel count.

saturday: Cold War concrete was crumbling in Europe while children everywhere stared down at their palms

puzzles.context_card: Competitors offered color screens, backlighting, and superior processors — and lost. The device's designer deliberately chose outdated screen technology because it consumed so little power that four AA batteries lasted 30 hours. Bundled with a puzzle game from the Soviet Union, it sold 118 million units and proved that portability mattered more than performance.

puzzles.headline: Black Tuesday Stock Crash
puzzles.description: The New York Stock Exchange lost roughly 12% of its value in a single session, accelerating a financial collapse that would define a generation's relationship with money.

saturday: Talkies had just killed the silent film star; now silence fell on the trading floor

puzzles.context_card: The ticker tape ran so far behind actual trades that brokers were selling stocks without knowing their real price. Sixteen million shares changed hands that day — a volume record that stood for nearly 40 years. The market wouldn't return to its pre-crash peak until 1954, a full quarter-century later.

puzzles.headline: First Photograph Taken
puzzles.description: A French inventor exposed a pewter plate coated in bitumen to light from his workshop window. The exposure took hours, capturing rooftops in a ghostly, revolutionary image.
puzzles.context_card: The exposure required roughly eight hours of direct sunlight, producing a grainy view of rooftops and a courtyard from an upstairs window in Burgundy. The inventor had been experimenting with light-sensitive bitumen for nearly a decade, failing repeatedly before this single surviving image. It now lives in a climate-controlled case at the University of Texas, so faded it can only be seen when lit at a precise angle.

puzzles.headline: First Telegraph Message Sent
puzzles.description: A message traveled instantaneously along a wire strung between Washington and Baltimore, compressing distance in a way humanity had never experienced. The words chosen were biblical.
puzzles.context_card: The message traveled 40 miles from the Capitol in Washington to a railroad depot in Baltimore in less than a second — a speed that made newspaper editors openly anxious about the future of their profession. Within a decade, wire stretched across the continent; within two, it crossed the Atlantic Ocean floor.

puzzles.headline: Printing Press Invented
puzzles.description: A German goldsmith in Mainz combined movable metal type, oil-based ink, and a wooden screw press, producing books at a pace that would dismantle the medieval monopoly on knowledge.
puzzles.context_card: The technology itself wasn't new — China had movable type four centuries earlier — but the innovation was combining it with a converted wine press, oil-based ink, and a metal alloy that could survive thousands of impressions. Within 50 years, an estimated 20 million volumes had been produced across Europe, more than all the scribes of the previous millennium combined.

puzzles.headline: American Civil War Begins
puzzles.description: Confederate batteries opened fire on a federal fort in Charleston Harbor before dawn, igniting the bloodiest conflict in American history after decades of sectional tension over slavery.

monday: Abraham Lincoln had been inaugurated just weeks before the first shots
The Pony Express was still carrying mail across the western frontier

puzzles.context_card: The 34-hour bombardment killed no one on either side — the only fatality came during the surrender ceremony, when a cannon exploded. The fort's commander had personally trained several of the Confederate officers now firing on him. Four years and 620,000 deaths later, he returned to raise the same flag he'd been forced to lower.

puzzles.headline: Spanish Flu Pandemic Begins
puzzles.description: A devastating influenza strain swept across a world still entangled in the Great War, eventually killing more people than the conflict itself.

monday: The Great War was grinding through its final year in Europe
Ragtime was giving way to early jazz in American dance halls

puzzles.context_card: It killed more people in 24 weeks than HIV/AIDS killed in 24 years, yet wartime censorship kept it out of headlines in combatant nations — only neutral Spain reported freely, which is how a Kansas-origin virus got a Spanish name. The second wave struck healthy adults aged 20-40 hardest. Global death estimates range from 50 to 100 million, making the true toll permanently unknowable.

puzzles.headline: Elvis Presley's First TV Appearance
puzzles.description: A young truck driver from Memphis performed on national television, and his swiveling hips scandalized parents while sending teenage viewership through the roof.

monday: The Interstate Highway System had just been authorized by Congress
The Hungarian Revolution was making front-page news that autumn

puzzles.context_card: CBS cameras were ordered to film from the waist up, but the damage was already done — his appearance weeks earlier had drawn the largest television audience in history. He earned $50,000 for three appearances, a staggering sum for a 21-year-old truck driver from Mississippi. The host privately called him 'a decent, fine boy' while publicly declaring the performance unsuitable for family television.

puzzles.headline: First McDonald's Franchise Opens
puzzles.description: A milkshake machine salesman opened a franchised hamburger stand in suburban Illinois, applying assembly-line efficiency to the American lunch counter.

tuesday: Disneyland opened its gates in Anaheim that same summer
Rosa Parks refused to give up her bus seat in Montgomery months later

puzzles.context_card: The franchising salesman who built the empire wasn't the founder — he was a 52-year-old milkshake machine vendor who walked into a San Bernardino hamburger stand and saw the future. The original brothers had already perfected their 'Speedee Service System,' serving a burger in 30 seconds. He bought them out for $2.7 million; their handshake deal for ongoing royalties was never honored.

puzzles.headline: Disneyland Opens
puzzles.description: An animator-turned-mogul converted 160 acres of Anaheim orange groves into a themed amusement park, inviting television cameras to broadcast opening day to millions.

tuesday: James Dean's final film was released that same year
The Salk polio vaccine had just been declared safe months earlier

puzzles.context_card: Opening day was such a disaster that insiders still call it 'Black Sunday' — temperatures hit 100°F, asphalt softened under women's heels, and counterfeit tickets doubled the crowd. A plumbers' strike forced a choice between working toilets and working water fountains; the creator chose toilets. The park recouped its entire $17 million construction cost within seven weeks.

puzzles.headline: First Barbie Doll Released
puzzles.description: A toy company executive introduced an 11.5-inch fashion doll at the American Toy Fair in New York, giving girls a plaything modeled on adult aspiration rather than infant care.

tuesday: Alaska and Hawaii became states that same year
Fidel Castro had just seized power in Havana weeks earlier

puzzles.context_card: She was inspired by a German novelty doll originally marketed to adult men as a gag gift. The creator's male business partners all argued that mothers would never buy their daughters a doll with breasts; 300,000 sold in the first year. Her full name — Barbara Millicent Roberts — was never printed on a box.

puzzles.headline: Pac-Man Released
puzzles.description: A Japanese arcade game starring a yellow disc that devoured dots in a maze became a global phenomenon, attracting players who had never touched a joystick.

wednesday: The designer wanted to create something women and couples would play on dates
The US boycotted that summer's Olympics in the host city

puzzles.context_card: The original Japanese name was changed for Western markets because distributors feared vandals would alter the first letter. It earned $2.5 billion in quarters within its first 15 months. The kill screen on level 256, caused by an integer overflow, was not beaten legitimately until 1999.

puzzles.headline: Super Mario Bros Released
puzzles.description: A mustachioed plumber sprinted across side-scrolling worlds on a home console, reviving a crashed video game industry and defining platforming for a generation.

wednesday: The North American console market had collapsed two years prior and retailers refused to stock games
Live Aid concerts raised millions for Ethiopian famine relief that same summer

puzzles.context_card: The game was designed as the pack-in title for a console that retailers initially refused to carry. Its creator had no formal programming training and composed the music himself. The game sold 40 million copies — without internet distribution — and single-handedly resurrected a home entertainment industry that analysts had declared permanently dead.

puzzles.headline: Tetris Released
puzzles.description: A Soviet computer scientist created a deceptively simple puzzle game involving falling geometric shapes. It escaped the Iron Curtain to become one of the most addictive programs ever written.

wednesday: Its creator worked at the Soviet Academy of Sciences and initially received no royalties
George Orwell's dystopian year had just passed with surprising calm

puzzles.context_card: The licensing rights became the subject of the most byzantine legal battle in gaming history — the Soviet government, a British software company, and multiple American publishers all claimed ownership simultaneously. Its designer earned nothing from the game's first decade of global sales. Cognitive scientists later discovered it literally changes brain structure, thickening the cortex in regions governing spatial reasoning.

puzzles.headline: PlayStation Launched
puzzles.description: A Japanese electronics giant — better known for Walkmans and televisions — entered the console market with a CD-based system that lured developers away from cartridge rivals.

thursday: The console was born from a failed partnership with its future rival's cartridge-based system

puzzles.context_card: It began as a CD-ROM add-on for a competitor's console, but the partnership collapsed in a public betrayal at a trade show. The jilted partner channeled that anger into a standalone machine that used cheaper disc media, undercut the competition by $100, and sold over 100 million units — inaugurating a new era of 3D gaming that made sprites feel instantly obsolete.

puzzles.headline: Xbox Announced
puzzles.description: A software company best known for office productivity and operating systems revealed its first video game console, challenging two entrenched Japanese competitors.

thursday: The September 11 attacks delayed the console's European launch by months

puzzles.context_card: The machine was essentially a stripped-down PC running a custom operating system, which horrified gaming purists. Internal memos reveal the company lost an estimated $125 on every unit sold, treating the entire venture as a $4 billion bet to control the living room. Its online service, bundled with an Ethernet port when competitors still used dial-up, would eventually reshape how the entire industry thought about multiplayer gaming.

puzzles.headline: First iPod Released
puzzles.description: A computer company unveiled a pocket-sized device promising a thousand songs in your pocket, pairing a tiny hard drive with a satisfying click wheel.

thursday: Portable MP3 players already existed, but this one used a tiny hard drive from a Japanese manufacturer

puzzles.context_card: Critics mocked the $399 price tag — 'no wireless, less space than a Nomad, lame,' read the most famous review. The device accounted for less than 2% of the company's revenue in its first year. Within six years it would generate half the company's income and lead directly to the product that would make it the world's most valuable corporation.

puzzles.headline: Netflix Streaming Launches
puzzles.description: A DVD-by-mail company began letting subscribers watch movies directly through their web browsers, betting that broadband speeds had finally caught up to the idea.

friday: The first iPhone shipped that same summer, giving streaming a mobile future

puzzles.context_card: The streaming library at launch contained just 1,000 titles — less than a single Blockbuster store. The company had offered to sell itself to its brick-and-mortar rival for $50 million three years earlier and been laughed out of the room. That rival filed for bankruptcy three years after streaming launched; the streamer is now worth more than $150 billion.

puzzles.headline: Spotify Launches
puzzles.description: A Swedish startup offered millions of songs on demand for free — legally — funded by advertising, challenging the decade-long piracy crisis that had gutted the music industry.

friday: The global financial crisis was unfolding as the service launched in Sweden

puzzles.context_card: It launched in invitation-only mode in a single European country, paying fractions of a cent per stream while the music industry was still prosecuting teenagers for illegal downloads. Major labels initially licensed their catalogs only because they were desperate — CD sales had fallen by half in a decade. The model musicians mocked as 'paying in pennies' now generates more revenue for the recording industry than CDs ever did at their peak.

puzzles.headline: Facebook Launched
puzzles.description: A Harvard sophomore opened a social networking site restricted to college students with .edu email addresses. Within weeks, it had spread to dozens of campuses.

friday: Gmail launched in invitation-only beta that same year

puzzles.context_card: Registration required a valid university email address, and the site spread campus by campus — a deliberate scarcity strategy that created desperate demand at schools not yet invited. Within 24 hours of launching at each new university, more than half the student body had signed up. The founder was 19 years old, running the servers from his dormitory room.

puzzles.headline: Twitter Launched
puzzles.description: A microblogging platform limited posts to 140 characters, distilling the internet's chatter into a real-time stream that would reshape journalism and political communication.

saturday: A 140-character whisper that turned every phone into a printing press

puzzles.context_card: The original prototype was built in two weeks as a side project at a podcasting startup, and the first message — 'just setting up my twttr' — had all the grandeur of a throat-clearing cough. The 140-character limit was dictated by SMS technology, not philosophy. It would take an earthquake in Haiti, an uprising in Tehran, and an Arab Spring to reveal what a short-message platform could actually become.

puzzles.headline: Snapchat Launched
puzzles.description: A messaging app built around photos that disappeared after viewing launched from a Stanford dorm room, inverting social media's assumption that everything should be permanent.

saturday: Born in a world where every photo was forever, promising that nothing had to be

puzzles.context_card: Every investor said an app built on impermanence was fundamentally worthless. A major social network offered $3 billion in cash to acquire it; the 23-year-old CEO declined. The feature everyone assumed was designed for sexting turned out to be what teenagers actually wanted: a way to communicate without building a permanent, searchable archive of their adolescence.

puzzles.headline: Bitcoin White Paper Published
puzzles.description: A pseudonymous author posted a nine-page paper to a cryptography mailing list describing a peer-to-peer electronic cash system that required no trusted third party.

saturday: Lehman Brothers had collapsed weeks before, and trust in banks was at a generational low

puzzles.context_card: The nine-page paper was posted to an obscure cryptography mailing list and received almost no attention. Embedded in the code of the first-ever block was a headline from The Times of London about bank bailouts — a quiet political statement disguised as a timestamp. The author vanished completely in 2011, leaving behind roughly one million tokens now worth tens of billions, never moved from their original addresses.

puzzles.headline: Ethereum Launches
puzzles.description: A blockchain network went live promising not just digital currency but programmable smart contracts — a world computer that anyone could build on without permission.
puzzles.context_card: Its creator was a 21-year-old college dropout who decided the original digital currency was too limited — like a calculator when the world needed a computer. He funded the project through a public token sale that raised $18 million in digital currency. The platform's name was chosen from a list of science fiction concepts found on Wikipedia.

puzzles.headline: First NFT Minted
puzzles.description: An artist created a unique digital token on a blockchain, attaching verifiable ownership to a piece of digital media in a way that had never been possible before.
puzzles.context_card: The first known experiment linked a short animation to a blockchain entry and declared it a unique, ownable digital object — a concept that sounded absurd to anyone who understood how copying a file works. The technology languished in obscurity for years before a $69 million auction made the art world pay attention in 2021. The inevitable crash wiped out 95% of values within two years.

puzzles.headline: Columbus Reaches the Americas
puzzles.description: A Genoese navigator sailing under the Spanish crown made landfall on a Caribbean island, believing he had reached Asia. The miscalculation changed the course of two hemispheres.
puzzles.context_card: The navigator died believing he had reached Asia, never acknowledging the continental landmass his voyages had revealed to European mapmakers. The Taíno people he encountered had inhabited the Caribbean for at least 1,500 years. Within 50 years of first contact, disease, enslavement, and violence had reduced the Taíno population from an estimated 250,000 to fewer than 500.

puzzles.headline: Magna Carta Signed
puzzles.description: English barons forced King John to seal a charter at Runnymede, limiting royal power for the first time — a document that would outlive every monarch who tried to ignore it.

monday: The Crusades were still being fought in the Holy Land
Oxford University had already been teaching for decades

puzzles.context_card: The meadow at Runnymede was chosen because neither side trusted the other enough to meet indoors. King John sealed the charter under duress, then immediately asked the Pope to annul it — which he did within ten weeks. The document failed as a peace treaty but survived as the foundation of constitutional law across the English-speaking world.

puzzles.headline: Shakespeare Born
puzzles.description: A glover's son arrived in Stratford-upon-Avon during a plague year, destined to give the English language more new words than any other writer before or since.

monday: Elizabeth I had recently been crowned queen
Galileo was a newborn in Pisa

puzzles.context_card: Born in a market town best known for its wool trade, the playwright received a solid grammar school education but never attended university — a fact his rivals never let him forget. He would go on to invent over 1,700 English words, from 'assassination' to 'lonely.' His father made gloves; his son died at eleven.

puzzles.headline: Star Wars Released
puzzles.description: A space opera that every studio in Hollywood had rejected finally reached theaters, and nothing about the movie business was ever quite the same.

monday: Jimmy Carter was in his first year as US president
The Apple II personal computer went on sale that same year

puzzles.context_card: Twentieth Century Fox was so certain the film would fail that it let the director keep merchandising rights — a decision that would cost the studio billions. Shot partly in the Tunisian desert, where a nearby Libyan military operation nearly shut down production, it earned $461 million worldwide and redefined what a movie could sell.

puzzles.headline: Jaws Released
puzzles.description: A mechanical shark that barely worked turned a young director's nightmare production into the first true summer blockbuster, and beach attendance dropped nationwide.

tuesday: The Watergate scandal had just forced a president to resign
Saturday Night Live premiered on NBC that same fall

puzzles.context_card: The mechanical shark malfunctioned so often that the young director was forced to hide it, showing only glimpses — a constraint that accidentally invented modern suspense filmmaking. Beaches across America emptied that summer despite record heat. The film's success created the template every studio still follows: wide release, massive marketing, summer launch.

puzzles.headline: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial Released
puzzles.description: Spielberg's gentle alien stranded in suburban California became the highest-grossing film of its era, proving that a puppet and a child actor could outperform any special effect.

tuesday: The Falklands War was being fought that same spring
Michael Jackson's Thriller album was released later that year

puzzles.context_card: The film was born from loneliness — the director conceived it while imagining an imaginary friend after his parents' divorce. M&M's famously declined a product placement deal, so the production used Reese's Pieces instead, tripling the candy's sales overnight. It became the highest-grossing film in history, a record it held for eleven years.

puzzles.headline: Jurassic Park Released
puzzles.description: Computer-generated dinosaurs sprinted across the screen for the first time, and practical effects artists everywhere felt a tremor that had nothing to do with a T. rex.

tuesday: The European Union was formally established that same year
Nirvana's In Utero album was released weeks later

puzzles.context_card: The novel's team pivoted from animatronics to an untested technique — computer-generated imagery for living creatures. The result — six minutes of CGI dinosaurs in a two-hour film — convinced every studio in Hollywood that practical effects were finished. The paleontology department at many universities saw enrollment double within a year.

puzzles.headline: The Matrix Released
puzzles.description: Bullet time, leather trenchcoats, and a red pill entered the cultural lexicon overnight, as two sibling directors reimagined what an action film could look like.

wednesday: The Y2K bug had the world bracing for digital collapse
The euro currency was introduced to financial markets that January

puzzles.context_card: The directors pitched the film with a comic book because no studio could visualize what they were describing. Every lead actor in Hollywood turned down the role before it was offered to a star best known for action comedies. The 'bullet time' effect required 120 still cameras firing in sequence — borrowed from a 19th-century photographer who used it to study galloping horses.

puzzles.headline: Avatar Released
puzzles.description: James Cameron bet everything on a film set on an alien moon, shot in 3D with technology that didn't exist when he started — and it became the highest-grossing movie in history.

wednesday: Barack Obama had just been inaugurated as US president
The world was deep in the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression

puzzles.context_card: The director waited fourteen years to make the film because the technology he needed did not exist yet. When it finally opened, it earned a billion dollars in just seventeen days. Its pioneering 3D technology was supposed to transform cinema forever; within five years, audiences had largely abandoned the format.

puzzles.headline: Sony Walkman Released
puzzles.description: A portable cassette player small enough to clip to your belt arrived from Tokyo, and for the first time, music became a truly private experience in public space.

wednesday: Margaret Thatcher had just become Britain's first female prime minister
The Soviet Union had recently invaded Afghanistan

puzzles.context_card: The device was rejected by every division inside the company before one engineer championed it. Its co-creator was so impatient with the prototype that he dunked it in an aquarium — when bubbles rose, he declared there was still space inside to make it smaller. It sold 200 million units and turned headphones from studio equipment into street fashion.

puzzles.headline: VHS Format Introduced
puzzles.description: A Japanese electronics company introduced a home video cassette format that would win a brutal standards war and turn every living room into a private cinema.

thursday: The United States was celebrating its bicentennial

puzzles.context_card: The format war between VHS and its rival Betamax had nothing to do with quality — Betamax was technically superior. VHS won because its tapes could record for two hours, long enough to capture a full movie, while Betamax initially maxed out at one. The adult film industry's early adoption of VHS may have tipped the balance, though the companies involved have always denied it.

puzzles.headline: CD Player Released
puzzles.description: A silver disc promising perfect sound forever went on sale, and vinyl purists immediately began an argument that still hasn't ended.

thursday: The Falklands War dominated the news that spring

puzzles.context_card: The disc's diameter — 12 centimeters — was allegedly chosen so it could hold all of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony without interruption. Early players cost more than $700, and only about fifty albums were available at launch. The format would eventually kill the vinyl record, then be killed itself by the MP3.

puzzles.headline: Kodak Camera Introduced
puzzles.description: George Eastman unveiled a simple box camera with the slogan 'You press the button, we do the rest' — photography was no longer reserved for professionals with darkrooms.

thursday: Jack the Ripper was terrorizing London's East End

puzzles.context_card: The founder's slogan was 'You press the button, we do the rest' — and he meant it literally. The camera came preloaded with a 100-exposure roll; when finished, customers mailed the entire device back to the factory. It democratized photography by removing every technical barrier, turning a specialist craft into a household habit.

puzzles.headline: Polaroid Camera Released
puzzles.description: Edwin Land's instant camera produced a finished photograph in sixty seconds, a trick so astonishing that people gathered in crowds to watch the image appear.

friday: The Berlin Airlift was keeping a city alive that same year

puzzles.context_card: The inventor's three-year-old daughter asked why she couldn't see the photo he had just taken of her — and that question launched a billion-dollar company. The first camera weighed nearly four pounds and produced sepia-toned prints that had to be coated with fixative by hand. Andy Warhol would later make the instant photograph an art form, carrying one everywhere he went.

puzzles.headline: Digital Camera Invented
puzzles.description: An engineer at Kodak cobbled together a toaster-sized device that captured an image on a digital sensor — his employer buried the invention, sensing it would destroy their film business.

friday: The Vietnam War had just ended with the fall of Saigon

puzzles.context_card: The prototype weighed eight pounds, took 23 seconds to record an image, and produced a 0.01 megapixel black-and-white photograph. The engineer who built it presented his invention to corporate leadership, who told him to bury it: the company's entire business was built on selling film. Three decades later, that same company filed for bankruptcy because digital photography had destroyed its market.

puzzles.headline: Ford Model T Released
puzzles.description: Henry Ford's affordable automobile rolled off the line in Detroit, and within a decade the horse-drawn carriage became a quaint relic of another century.

friday: The Wright brothers' first flight was still recent memory

puzzles.context_card: The assembly line did not start with the car — it started with the price. The founder was obsessed with making a car cheap enough for his own factory workers to buy, and the moving assembly line was simply the method that got the price from $850 down to $260. By the time production ended, half of all cars on American roads were the same model.

puzzles.headline: First Commercial Airplane Flight
puzzles.description: A paying passenger climbed aboard a Benoist flying boat in St. Petersburg, Florida, for a twenty-three-minute trip across Tampa Bay — the airline industry's very first ticket.

saturday: The old world was about to tear itself apart

puzzles.context_card: The flight lasted 23 minutes, covered 21 miles across a bay, and the only passenger was the mayor of the destination city, who had won the seat at auction. The pilot made the trip twice daily for four months before the airline folded — there simply were not enough passengers. It would take another decade before commercial aviation became a real industry.

puzzles.headline: Sputnik Launched
puzzles.description: A polished metal sphere the size of a beach ball began circling the Earth, its steady beep audible on ham radios worldwide — and the space race was on.

saturday: Leave It to Beaver was America's picture of itself

puzzles.context_card: The satellite was the size of a beach ball and carried no scientific instruments — it could only transmit a simple radio pulse. But that beep, picked up by amateur radio operators worldwide, proved that the rival superpower had rockets powerful enough to reach any city on Earth. Within a year, the stunned response produced NASA, a new science curriculum for American schools, and the modern concept of the technology race.

puzzles.headline: Hubble Space Telescope Launched
puzzles.description: NASA placed a school-bus-sized telescope in orbit, only to discover its primary mirror had been ground to the wrong shape — a flaw that would require the most ambitious repair in space history.

saturday: The wall between East and West had just crumbled

puzzles.context_card: The telescope was delivered to orbit with a flaw in its primary mirror — a manufacturing error of just 2.2 micrometers, about one-fiftieth the width of a human hair. For three years it sent back blurry images. Astronauts installed corrective optics during a spacewalk, and the telescope went on to fundamentally change humanity's understanding of the age and size of the universe.

puzzles.headline: Mars Pathfinder Landing
puzzles.description: A small rover named Sojourner rolled off a landing platform onto Martian soil, beaming back photographs of a rust-colored landscape no wheel had ever touched.
puzzles.context_card: The rover was the size of a microwave oven and was designed to last seven days. It lasted eighty-three. Named through a student essay contest, the little machine analyzed rocks and sent back 2.3 billion bits of data, proving Mars had once been warm and wet. The mission cost less than the production budget of a Hollywood blockbuster released that same summer.

puzzles.headline: Gutenberg Bible Printed
puzzles.description: A goldsmith in Mainz produced the first major book printed with movable metal type, a technical feat that would dismantle the Church's monopoly on knowledge within a generation.
puzzles.context_card: The printer was nearly bankrupt before the first Bible rolled off his press — he had borrowed heavily and his investor eventually sued him, seizing the equipment. The original print run was about 180 copies, of which 49 survive today, each worth tens of millions of dollars. Within fifty years, an estimated twenty million volumes had been printed across Europe, ending the Church's monopoly on knowledge.

puzzles.headline: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
puzzles.description: A teenage emperor was deposed by a Germanic chieftain, and the political entity that had governed western Europe for centuries quietly ceased to exist.
puzzles.context_card: The final emperor was a teenager, installed as a puppet by his father, a Germanic general who actually held the power. The barbarian king who deposed him did not even bother to claim the title — he simply sent the imperial regalia to Constantinople and declared the western office unnecessary. The eastern half of the empire would continue for nearly another thousand years.

puzzles.headline: Great Fire of London
puzzles.description: A bakery fire on Pudding Lane spread through timber-framed streets for four days, leveling most of the medieval city — and inadvertently ending the plague that had ravaged it the year before.

monday: The Great Plague had devastated the same city just a year before
Isaac Newton was a young student developing his theory of gravity

puzzles.context_card: The fire began in a bakery on Pudding Lane shortly after midnight and burned for four days, destroying 13,200 houses, 87 churches, and old St Paul's Cathedral. Remarkably, only six verified deaths were recorded. The architect appointed to rebuild the city designed 51 new churches, including a cathedral that took 35 years to complete.

puzzles.event: The Beatles' First US TV Appearance
puzzles.event_formal: The Beatles on The Ed Sullivan Show
puzzles.headline: The Beatles on Ed Sullivan
puzzles.description: Four young men from Liverpool performed on American television to a studio audience of screaming teenagers, and seventy-three million viewers at home watched the culture shift in real time.

monday: The president had been assassinated just weeks before
Martin Luther King Jr. would receive the Nobel Peace Prize later that year

puzzles.context_card: When The Beatles landed at JFK Airport on February 7, 1964, they were met by 3,000 screaming fans — the largest welcome in airport history to that point. They appeared on The Ed Sullivan Show ten days later, watched by 73 million viewers. Within weeks, Beatlemania had completely reshaped American pop music and youth culture.

puzzles.headline: Woodstock Festival
puzzles.description: Half a million people descended on a dairy farm in upstate New York for three days of music and mud, creating the defining gathering of the counterculture era.

monday: Humans had walked on the moon just weeks earlier
Richard Nixon was in his first year as president

puzzles.context_card: The festival was supposed to happen in a different town entirely — the original site pulled the permit a month before the event. A dairy farmer agreed to lease his land, and within weeks his alfalfa field became a temporary city with no fences and no way to collect tickets. The promoters lost over a million dollars. The farmer said he would do it again.

puzzles.headline: Live Aid Concert
puzzles.description: Bob Geldof organized a simultaneous rock concert in London and Philadelphia, broadcast to nearly two billion viewers, turning stadium music into a vehicle for global famine relief.

tuesday: A hole in the ozone layer was discovered that same year
Nintendo released its first home console in North America that October

puzzles.context_card: The concert was organized in ten weeks — an impossibly short timeline for an event broadcast live to 1.9 billion people across 150 countries. The London and Philadelphia stages ran simultaneously, connected by satellite. It raised $127 million for famine relief, but its lasting impact was proving that television could turn a concert into a global event and shame governments into action.

puzzles.headline: MTV Launches
puzzles.description: A new cable channel began broadcasting music videos around the clock, opening with a Buggles clip whose title turned out to be prophetic — 'Video Killed the Radio Star.'

tuesday: The first Space Shuttle mission launched that same spring
Prince Charles married Lady Diana that summer

puzzles.context_card: The first video ever played was a deliberate joke — a song about how television had disrupted an earlier medium. At launch, the channel could only be received in a few thousand homes in New Jersey. Record labels initially refused to provide videos, so the same handful of clips ran on heavy rotation for months. Within three years, an artist's visual image had become as important as their sound.

puzzles.headline: Napster Launches
puzzles.description: A college dropout's file-sharing program let anyone with an internet connection download almost any song for free, and the music industry's business model collapsed virtually overnight.

tuesday: The Y2K bug dominated headlines that December
The Sopranos had just premiered on HBO

puzzles.context_card: Two college freshmen built the service in a dorm room, and within a year it had more users than most countries have people. The recording industry sued, calling it the biggest threat to copyright since the photocopier. The legal battle shut it down, but the damage was done — an entire generation had learned that music could be free, and the industry spent the next decade trying to unlearn that lesson.

puzzles.headline: iTunes Store Opens
puzzles.description: Apple offered legal music downloads for ninety-nine cents per song, giving the recording industry a lifeline after years of rampant piracy.

wednesday: The US had invaded Iraq weeks earlier
The Human Genome Project was completed that same spring

puzzles.context_card: The music industry had spent four years suing teenagers and losing the public relations battle when a tech company proposed a radical idea: make buying music easier than stealing it. The store sold one million songs in its first five days. Within six years, it was the largest music retailer in the United States, and CDs were relics.

puzzles.headline: The Berlin Wall Falls
puzzles.description: East German border guards, overwhelmed by crowds and confused by a bungled press conference, stepped aside — and thousands poured through checkpoints sealed for a generation.

wednesday: The Tiananmen Square protests had been crushed just months before
The first episode of The Simpsons aired that December

puzzles.context_card: For 28 years, Checkpoint Charlie was the defining symbol of the Cold War. On November 9th, 1989, a confused bureaucrat announced that East Germans could cross freely — and thousands flooded through. No one had planned the announcement. The wall fell because the system was already crumbling.

puzzles.headline: World War I Begins
puzzles.description: The assassination of an archduke in Sarajevo triggered a chain of alliances and ultimatums that, within weeks, plunged the great powers of Europe into the most devastating conflict the world had yet seen.

wednesday: The Panama Canal had just opened for business
Charlie Chaplin made his first film appearance that same year

puzzles.context_card: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914, set off a chain reaction of alliance obligations. What was supposed to be a short war became a four-year conflict that killed 17 million people and destroyed four empires: the German, Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, and Russian empires.
